title: Fevers and chills after a trip abroad

chart.cc: Fevers, chills, and headache since returning from travel
chart.oneLiner: 34M with type 2 diabetes (on metformin), HIV on antiretroviral therapy, and G6PD deficiency presents with 5 days of fevers, rigors, headache, and myalgias that began about a week after returning from rural West Africa, where he stopped his malaria prophylaxis early.

opening: I keep getting these terrible fevers and shaking chills since I got back from my trip, and my whole body aches.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 34-year-old man, flushed and diaphoretic, looks unwell and uncomfortable but is alert and a good historian. He is worried because the fevers keep coming back and he feels weaker each day.

HPI: 5 days of fevers reaching 39-40°C with drenching sweats and bone-shaking rigors; at first they came roughly every other day but for the last 2 days they are almost continuous. Severe frontal headache, diffuse myalgias, profound fatigue, poor appetite, and several episodes of nausea, vomiting, and loose non-bloody stools. He noticed his urine looked darker today (only if asked about urine color/output). Returned ~9 days ago from a 3-week trip to rural Nigeria (West Africa) visiting family (only if asked about travel). Took a few days of malaria prophylaxis early in the trip but stopped because of nausea and did NOT complete it (only if asked specifically about prophylaxis/pills to prevent malaria). Slept in a village without bed nets or air conditioning and had many mosquito bites (only if asked about mosquito exposure/precautions). He ate local food and drank village well water (only if asked about food/water). NO cough, NO dysuria, NO neck stiffness, NO rash, NO obvious bleeding, NO confusion currently. No sick contacts with similar illness, though a cousin he visited had a prolonged fever last month (only if asked about sick contacts).
PMH (reveal when asked about medical history): (1) Type 2 diabetes for 4 years, last A1c ~8.6%, sometimes runs low when he skips meals. (2) HIV diagnosed 6 years ago, on antiretroviral therapy, says his last CD4 was 'around 350' and viral load undetectable. (3) Known G6PD deficiency (found years ago after a reaction to an antibiotic) — he was specifically told to avoid certain malaria pills and 'sulfa' drugs (reveal only if asked about blood disorders, prior drug reactions, or G6PD).
MEDS (reveal when asked about medications): metformin twice daily; a single-tablet HIV antiretroviral regimen; ondansetron taken for the nausea on this trip; citalopram for anxiety; he stopped the antimalarial prophylaxis early. ALLERGIES: had hemolysis/dark urine after a sulfa antibiotic years ago (G6PD-related). SOCIAL: works as an engineer; minimal alcohol; no injection drug use; monogamous, one partner. IMMUNIZATIONS: yellow fever and typhoid vaccines before travel, hepatitis A complete (if asked about vaccines). FAMILY: noncontributory.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: ill-appearing, flushed, diaphoretic, febrile; alert and fully oriented (NO confusion/obtundation).
- Vitals: BP 98/60, HR 122, RR 24, 39.6°C, SpO2 95% RA.
- Abdomen: mild splenomegaly and mild tenderness in the left upper quadrant if examined; no peritoneal signs.
- HEENT/Skin: mild scleral icterus and pale conjunctivae if examined; NO rash, NO petechiae, NO eschar, NO rose spots, NO frank jaundice of the skin.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur; mildly dry mucous membranes.
- Neuro: alert and oriented, no meningismus, no focal deficit.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the travel history, incomplete prophylaxis, mosquito exposure, food/water source, or the sick cousin unless asked about the corresponding topic.
- Do NOT volunteer the diabetes, HIV, G6PD deficiency, or the medication list unless asked about past medical history, blood disorders/drug reactions, or medications respectively.
- Splenomegaly, scleral icterus, and pale conjunctivae are revealed only if the abdomen/eyes/conjunctivae are examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.BloodSmear: Thick and thin Giemsa-stained blood smears: intraerythrocytic ring-form trophozoites of Plasmodium falciparum with occasional banana-shaped gametocytes; multiply-infected red cells seen; parasitemia ~6.5% on the thin film (quantified, >5% — a SEVERE criterion). No Schuffner dots. Speciation favors falciparum.
labs.RDT: Rapid diagnostic test POSITIVE for P. falciparum HRP-2 antigen and NEGATIVE for the pan-Plasmodium (pLDH) band pattern of non-falciparum species — confirms falciparum while the smear is quantified.
labs.CBC: Hgb 8.4 (down from a baseline ~13), MCV 92, reticulocytes elevated, haptoglobin low and LDH high (hemolytic picture). Plt 58 (thrombocytopenia). WBC 5.6, normal differential (NO leukocytosis — argues against pyogenic bacterial sepsis).
labs.Chemistry: Na 134, K 3.1 (hypokalemia from vomiting/diarrhea), HCO3 18 with an anion gap of 18 (Na 134 − Cl 98 − HCO3 18 = 18; gap metabolic acidosis). Cr 2.1 (baseline ~0.9 — ACUTE kidney injury). Glucose 58 (HYPOGLYCEMIA). Lactate 3.6 (elevated). Total bilirubin 4.2 with indirect (unconjugated) predominance from hemolysis; AST/ALT mildly elevated.
labs.BloodCultures: Bacterial blood cultures and stool/blood Salmonella workup drawn and PENDING — NO growth to date (helps exclude typhoid/enteric fever and bacterial sepsis as the primary cause).
labs.G6PDLevel: Confirmed G6PD deficiency on quantitative assay (drawn during a non-acute window is preferred, but historical deficiency is documented) — contraindicates primaquine/tafenoquine and sulfa-based agents because of hemolysis risk.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia with a markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~510 ms). This patient's OWN tracing: the long QT is driven by hypokalemia (K 3.1) PLUS QT-prolonging medications (ondansetron, citalopram) — a dangerous baseline BEFORE adding any QT-prolonging antimalarial (quinine, chloroquine, lumefantrine). No acute ischemic ST changes.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: sinus tachycardia with drug/electrolyte-induced QT prolongation (long QTc). Self-host with appropriate credit.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing sinus tachycardia with a prolonged QT interval (drug- and hypokalemia-related)
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 34-year-old man returning from the tropics with fever, vomiting, and several QT-prolonging medications has an EKG before antimalarial therapy is chosen.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Clear lung fields with no consolidation or effusion; normal cardiomediastinal silhouette. New bilateral infiltrates would raise concern for malaria-associated ARDS/pulmonary edema, a feature of SEVERE malaria — a pertinent negative to document at baseline given his other severe criteria.
cxr.verify: Source a CC chest film that is clear/normal, OR one showing bilateral infiltrates (ARDS); label which it is. Self-host with appropriate credit.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph with clear lung fields (screening for malaria-associated ARDS)
cxr.clinicalVignette: A traveler back from West Africa with fevers and mild breathlessness has a screening chest film.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical in this febrile returning traveler from West Africa, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis and the single best competing alternative). Then name THREE mechanisms by which your leading diagnosis causes severe, multi-system disease.
steps.rubric: Malaria — leading & CANT-MISS (8). Typhoid/enteric fever — the genuine competing diagnosis given village well water, GI symptoms, the febrile contact, relative bradycardia/normal WBC, and an incompletely protective typhoid vaccine; must be excluded (CANT-MISS,5). Viral hemorrhagic fever / arboviral illness (dengue) (core,2). Bacterial sepsis/occult bacteremia, higher pretest given HIV (core,2). Acute viral hepatitis / leptospirosis / rickettsial or other cosmopolitan infection (core,1). Connects fever pattern + endemic travel + incomplete prophylaxis + splenomegaly + thrombocytopenia/hemolysis to malaria and states malaria must be excluded urgently in ANY febrile traveler (core,3). Names THREE mechanisms of severe falciparum disease — e.g. cytoadherence/sequestration of infected RBCs in microvasculature, intravascular hemolysis with anemia, and metabolic derangement (hypoglycemia/lactic acidosis); also acceptable: AKI/blackwater, cerebral malaria, ARDS, DIC (core,4). PENALTY: anchoring on malaria and never working up typhoid/bacteremia despite the well-water and HIV history (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Malaria (incomplete prophylaxis in West Africa, cyclical-then-continuous fevers, splenomegaly, thrombocytopenia, hemolytic anemia). Single best competing alternative to exclude: Typhoid/enteric fever — he drank village well water, has prominent GI symptoms, a febrile household contact, a normal WBC, and his typhoid vaccine is only partially protective. Also reasonable: viral hemorrhagic fever / arboviral illness (dengue); bacterial sepsis/occult bacteremia (higher pretest given HIV); acute viral hepatitis, leptospirosis, or rickettsial disease. Malaria must be excluded urgently in any febrile traveler. Three mechanisms of severe falciparum disease: (1) cytoadherence and microvascular sequestration of parasitized RBCs (cerebral malaria, organ ischemia); (2) intravascular hemolysis and rosetting causing anemia and AKI/blackwater; (3) host metabolic derangement — hypoglycemia and lactic (anion-gap) acidosis.

coreActions.item: Viral hemorrhagic fever / arboviral illness (dengue)
Bacterial sepsis/occult bacteremia (higher pretest given HIV)
Acute viral hepatitis / leptospirosis / rickettsial or other cosmopolitan infection
Connects fever pattern + endemic travel + incomplete prophylaxis + splenomegaly + thrombocytopenia/hemolysis to malaria and states it must be excluded urgently
Names three mechanisms of severe falciparum disease (sequestration/cytoadherence; hemolysis/anemia/AKI; hypoglycemia/lactic acidosis)

penalties.item: Anchors on malaria and never works up typhoid/bacteremia despite well-water and HIV history

acceptableAlternatives: Acute viral hepatitis / leptospirosis / rickettsial or other cosmopolitan infection

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Thick and thin blood smears for malaria parasites with species ID and parasitemia (CANT-MISS,4). Rapid diagnostic test (RDT) for malaria antigen (CANT-MISS,3). CBC with platelets + hemolysis labs (retic/LDH/haptoglobin) (2). Chemistry with glucose, lactate, bilirubin, and renal function to grade SEVERE malaria (2). Blood cultures + stool/blood Salmonella studies to exclude typhoid/bacterial sepsis (2). G6PD level before any 8-aminoquinoline given his history (1). Repeat smears q12-24h x3 if first negative (1). PENALTY: ruling out malaria on a single negative smear without repeating (-2); shotgun panel with no severity assessment (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG (he is on QT-prolonging drugs and may need a QT-prolonging antimalarial); CXR; thick and thin blood smears with species ID and parasitemia; RDT; CBC with platelets plus hemolysis labs (reticulocytes, LDH, haptoglobin); Chemistry including glucose, lactate, bilirubin, and creatinine to grade severity; blood cultures and stool/blood Salmonella studies to exclude typhoid/bacteremia; and a G6PD level before any primaquine/tafenoquine given his documented deficiency. Repeat smears q12-24h x3 if the first is negative.

criticalActions.item: Thick and thin blood smears with species ID and parasitemia
Rapid diagnostic test (RDT) for malaria antigen

coreActions.item: EKG
CXR
CBC with platelets plus hemolysis labs (retic/LDH/haptoglobin)
Chemistry with glucose, lactate, bilirubin, and renal function to grade severity
Blood cultures plus stool/blood Salmonella studies to exclude typhoid/bacteremia
G6PD level before any 8-aminoquinoline

penalties.item: ruling out malaria on a single negative smear without repeating q12-24h x3

requiredTests: Thick and thin blood smears with species ID and parasitemia
Rapid diagnostic test (RDT) for malaria antigen
contraindicatedTests: ruling out malaria on a single negative smear without repeating q12-24h x3
orderSensitiveActions: ruling out malaria on a single negative smear without repeating q12-24h x3

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and state how they change your treatment plan.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia with a markedly PROLONGED QT (QTc ~510 ms). CREDIT: rate & rhythm — sinus tachycardia (4); explicitly identifies the prolonged QT/long QTc (5); attributes it to hypokalemia (K 3.1) PLUS QT-prolonging meds (ondansetron, citalopram) (3); states this mandates correcting potassium/magnesium and choosing/monitoring antimalarial therapy carefully because quinine/chloroquine/lumefantrine further prolong QT and risk torsades (3). PENALTY: missing the long QT and starting a QT-prolonging antimalarial without correcting electrolytes or monitoring (-3).
keyFindings: markedly prolonged QT / long QTc (~510 ms)
attributes QT prolongation to hypokalemia plus QT-prolonging medications (ondansetron, citalopram)
recognizes risk of torsades if a QT-prolonging antimalarial is added without correcting electrolytes
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia with a markedly prolonged QT (QTc ~510 ms) from hypokalemia plus QT-prolonging medications — correct K+/Mg2+ and avoid stacking QT-prolonging antimalarials without monitoring.
commonMistakes: missing the prolonged QT and stacking a QT-prolonging antimalarial on top
reading it as a benign sinus tachycardia only
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia with a markedly prolonged QT interval (QTc ~510 ms). The long QT is driven by hypokalemia (K 3.1) plus his QT-prolonging medications (ondansetron, citalopram). This is the patient's own baseline before therapy: I must replete potassium and magnesium and be very cautious adding a QT-prolonging antimalarial (quinine, chloroquine, lumefantrine), with continuous telemetry and serial QTc — IV artesunate (preferred here for severe disease) does not prolong QT and is the safer backbone.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: sinus tachycardia with a markedly prolonged QT (QTc ~510 ms) from hypokalemia plus QT-prolonging medications, mandating electrolyte correction before/with any QT-prolonging antimalarial.

coreActions.item: rate & rhythm (sinus tachycardia)
attributes the prolonged QT to hypokalemia plus ondansetron/citalopram
states the need to correct K+/Mg2+ and monitor QTc / avoid stacking QT-prolonging antimalarials (torsades risk)

penalties.item: missing the long QT and starting a QT-prolonging antimalarial without correcting electrolytes or monitoring

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = clear lungs; new bilateral infiltrates would suggest malaria-associated ARDS (a severe feature). CREDIT: identifies a clear/normal film (6); recognizes that bilateral infiltrates would indicate malaria-associated ARDS / severe disease and that this is a baseline pertinent negative given his other severe criteria (4). PENALTY: inventing pneumonia/consolidation that is not present (-2).
keyFindings: recognizes that bilateral infiltrates would indicate malaria-associated ARDS / severe disease
criticalFindings: Clear lung fields with no consolidation or effusion; new bilateral infiltrates would raise concern for malaria-associated ARDS/pulmonary edema (severe malaria) — a pertinent negative to document at baseline.
commonMistakes: inventing pneumonia/consolidation that is not present
steps.idealAnswer: Clear lung fields with no consolidation or effusion and a normal cardiomediastinal silhouette. New bilateral infiltrates would raise concern for malaria-associated ARDS/pulmonary edema, a feature of severe malaria — worth documenting as a baseline pertinent negative given his other severe criteria.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: clear lung fields with no consolidation or effusion; new bilateral infiltrates would indicate malaria-associated ARDS (severe malaria).

coreActions.item: identifies a clear/normal film
recognizes that bilateral infiltrates would indicate malaria-associated ARDS / severe disease

penalties.item: inventing pneumonia/consolidation that is not present

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: The smear shows P. falciparum ring forms at ~6.5% parasitemia (RDT positive for HRP-2), with Hgb 8.4 and a hemolytic picture, Plt 58, Cr 2.1 (baseline 0.9), glucose 58, lactate 3.6, HCO3 18 with an anion gap of 18, and the EKG shows a long QT. Blood cultures are no-growth so far. What is your diagnosis, is it severe, and what did you rule out? Name the single discriminating test that settled malaria vs typhoid.
steps.rubric: Malaria — Plasmodium falciparum, classified as SEVERE (6). Justifies severe by multiple criteria: parasitemia >5% (6.5%), AKI (Cr 2.1 from 0.9), hypoglycemia (glucose 58), and an anion-gap lactic acidosis (gap 18, lactate 3.6, HCO3 18) on a background of hemolytic anemia and thrombocytopenia. Names the positive thick/thin smear + falciparum-specific RDT (HRP-2) as the discriminating test that confirmed malaria and, with no-growth cultures, argues against typhoid/bacteremia; notes the long QT and G6PD deficiency now constrain drug choice (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Diagnosis: malaria due to Plasmodium falciparum, and it is SEVERE — not uncomplicated. Severity is established by parasitemia >5% (6.5%), acute kidney injury (Cr 2.1 from a baseline 0.9), hypoglycemia (glucose 58), and a high-anion-gap lactic acidosis (Na 134 − Cl 98 − HCO3 18 = gap 18, lactate 3.6), all superimposed on hemolytic anemia (Hgb 8.4, low haptoglobin, high LDH, indirect hyperbilirubinemia) and thrombocytopenia. The discriminating test was the positive thick/thin blood smear with a falciparum-specific RDT (HRP-2 positive, non-falciparum pLDH negative); with blood cultures showing no growth, this confirms malaria and argues against typhoid/enteric fever and bacteremia. Two confounders now shape therapy: the long QT (hypokalemia plus ondansetron/citalopram) and his documented G6PD deficiency.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including disposition and a clear safe-discharge/follow-up plan when he is ready to leave the hospital?
steps.rubric: Per MGH approach to malaria: confirm species/parasitemia and classify severity FIRST (CANT-MISS,4); because this is SEVERE falciparum, give IV ARTESUNATE and admit to a monitored/ICU setting — do NOT use oral ACT as primary therapy for severe disease (CANT-MISS,6); correct hypokalemia and hypoglycemia and obtain continuous telemetry / serial QTc given the long QT, and prefer artesunate (no QT effect) over quinine; hold/avoid additional QT-prolonging drugs where possible (4); manage AKI/hemolysis with careful fluids and monitor for blackwater/transfusion need (2); AVOID primaquine/tafenoquine and sulfa agents because of G6PD deficiency — falciparum has no hypnozoite stage so primaquine is not needed for radical cure anyway (2); Infectious Disease consult and notify public health (2); transition to a full oral ACT course once stable and tolerating PO, and arrange SAFE DISCHARGE — confirm clinical/parasitologic improvement, medication reconciliation (resume ART, restart metformin only when eating and renal function recovers, review QT-prolonging meds), counsel on prophylaxis adherence and bed nets, and ensure ID + primary-care follow-up with repeat smear (3). PENALTY: treating SEVERE falciparum with oral therapy / chloroquine monotherapy from a resistant region (-3); giving primaquine or a sulfa drug in a G6PD-deficient patient (-2); discharging before parasitemia falls and the patient is stable / without follow-up (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Confirm species and quantify parasitemia and classify severity first — this is SEVERE falciparum, so the cornerstone is IV ARTESUNATE with admission to a monitored/ICU bed; oral ACT alone is NOT adequate for severe disease. Correct hypokalemia and hypoglycemia immediately, place on continuous telemetry with serial QTc, and prefer artesunate (no QT effect) while avoiding/holding additional QT-prolonging drugs (ondansetron, citalopram, and QT-prolonging antimalarials like quinine/lumefantrine) given the QTc ~510 ms. Support the AKI and hemolysis with careful IV fluids, monitor for blackwater fever and the need for transfusion, and trend parasitemia. AVOID primaquine, tafenoquine, and sulfa-containing agents because of his G6PD deficiency — and note falciparum has no liver hypnozoites, so primaquine radical cure is not required. Infectious Disease consult and notify public health. Once stable, afebrile, and parasitemia is falling, transition to a complete oral ACT course (e.g., artemether-lumefantrine, watching QT) and arrange SAFE DISCHARGE: confirm parasitologic/clinical improvement, reconcile medications (resume ART, restart metformin only when eating and renal function recovers, minimize QT-prolonging drugs), counsel on completing prophylaxis and using bed nets for future travel, and ensure ID and primary-care follow-up with a repeat smear. Do NOT use chloroquine monotherapy for falciparum from a resistant region.

criticalActions.item: Confirm species/parasitemia and classify severity first
IV artesunate with ICU/monitored admission for SEVERE falciparum (not oral ACT alone)

coreActions.item: Correct hypokalemia/hypoglycemia, telemetry and serial QTc, prefer artesunate over QT-prolonging agents given long QT
Manage AKI/hemolysis with careful fluids and monitor for blackwater/transfusion need
Avoid primaquine/tafenoquine and sulfa agents given G6PD deficiency (falciparum has no hypnozoites)
Infectious Disease consult and public health notification
Transition to full oral ACT course when stable and arrange safe discharge: med reconciliation, prophylaxis/bed-net counseling, ID + primary-care follow-up with repeat smear

bonusActions.item: Counsel on prophylaxis adherence and bed nets for future travel
Recognizes HIV/diabetes raise the risk of severe disease and warrant closer monitoring

penalties.item: Treating severe falciparum with oral therapy or chloroquine monotherapy from a resistant region
Giving primaquine or a sulfa drug in a G6PD-deficient patient
Discharging before parasitemia falls / patient stable, or without follow-up

IV artesunate with ICU/monitored admission for SEVERE falciparum (not oral ACT alone)

Manage AKI/hemolysis with careful fluids and monitor for blackwater/transfusion need

Counsel on prophylaxis adherence and bed nets for future travel
Recognizes HIV/diabetes raise the risk of severe disease and warrant closer monitoring

physicalExamMappings.finding: Abdomen: mild splenomegaly and mild tenderness in the left upper quadrant if examined; no peritoneal signs.
HEENT/Skin: mild scleral icterus if examined; no rash, no petechiae, no eschar, no rose spots.
HEENT: pale conjunctivae consistent with anemia if examined; sclerae mildly icteric.
General: ill-appearing, flushed, diaphoretic, febrile; alert and fully oriented.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Patient is alert and in no acute distress.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Neuro: alert and oriented, no meningismus, no focal deficit (NO confusion/obtundation to suggest cerebral malaria).
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Alert and oriented; mental status grossly normal.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 34M with type 2 diabetes, HIV on ART, and G6PD deficiency presents with 5 days of fevers, rigors, headache, and myalgias that began about a week after returning from rural West Africa with incomplete malaria prophylaxis. The diagnosis is SEVERE Plasmodium falciparum malaria, reached by eliciting the focused travel/exposure and comorbidity history, performing the targeted exam (splenomegaly, scleral icterus, pale conjunctivae, normal mental status), and interpreting a directed workup that reveals multi-system involvement — hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, AKI, hypoglycemia, and an anion-gap lactic acidosis — while the falciparum-specific smear/RDT discriminates malaria from the leading competitor, typhoid/enteric fever, and a drug/electrolyte-related long QT and G6PD deficiency constrain therapy.
keyHistoryClues: travel to a malaria-endemic region (West Africa) with incomplete prophylaxis
village well water and a febrile household contact (raise typhoid)
HIV, diabetes, and G6PD deficiency that alter severity and drug choice
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: mild splenomegaly and mild tenderness in the left upper quadrant if examined; no peritoneal signs.
HEENT/Skin: mild scleral icterus if examined; no rash, no petechiae, no eschar, no rose spots.
HEENT: pale conjunctivae consistent with anemia if examined; sclerae mildly icteric.
Neuro: alert and oriented, no meningismus, no focal deficit (NO confusion/obtundation to suggest cerebral malaria).
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items (travel, prophylaxis, exposures, HIV, G6PD/drug reactions) only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings (splenomegaly, scleral icterus, pale conjunctivae, normal mental status).
Build a differential led by Malaria with typhoid/enteric fever as the genuine competing diagnosis, and name mechanisms of severe falciparum disease.
Order a focused workup (thick/thin smears, falciparum-specific RDT, hemolysis and severity labs, cultures, G6PD) and interpret the long-QT EKG and CXR.
Commit to SEVERE falciparum malaria, treat with IV artesunate, correct electrolytes/glucose, avoid primaquine/sulfa given G6PD, consult ID, and arrange safe discharge with follow-up.
commonPitfalls: Treating severe falciparum with oral therapy or chloroquine monotherapy from a resistant region
Giving primaquine or a sulfa drug in a G6PD-deficient patient
Stacking a QT-prolonging antimalarial on a long QT without correcting electrolytes
Ruling out malaria on a single negative smear without repeating
managementPearls: Classify severity first; severe falciparum gets IV artesunate, not oral ACT
Correct hypokalemia/hypoglycemia and monitor QTc given drug- and electrolyte-related QT prolongation
criticalWorkupItems: Thick and thin blood smears with species ID and parasitemia
Rapid diagnostic test (RDT) for malaria antigen
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia with a markedly prolonged QT (QTc ~510 ms) from hypokalemia plus QT-prolonging medications — correct K+/Mg2+ and avoid stacking QT-prolonging antimalarials without monitoring.
Clear lung fields with no consolidation or effusion; new bilateral infiltrates would indicate malaria-associated ARDS (severe malaria).
criticalManagementItems: Confirm species/parasitemia and classify severity first
IV artesunate with ICU/monitored admission for SEVERE falciparum (not oral ACT alone)
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with a markedly prolonged QT (QTc ~510 ms) from hypokalemia plus QT-prolonging medications — correct K+/Mg2+ and avoid stacking QT-prolonging antimalarials without monitoring.
Clear lung fields with no consolidation or effusion; new bilateral infiltrates would indicate malaria-associated ARDS (severe malaria).
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with a markedly prolonged QT (QTc ~510 ms) from hypokalemia plus QT-prolonging medications — correct K+/Mg2+ and avoid stacking QT-prolonging antimalarials without monitoring.
Clear lung fields with no consolidation or effusion; new bilateral infiltrates would indicate malaria-associated ARDS (severe malaria).
imageInterpretationPearls: The EKG here is the patient's OWN study: name the long QT, then judge its therapeutic relevance before choosing an antimalarial.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives — a clear CXR is a baseline pertinent negative for malaria-associated ARDS.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Asks permission before sensitive questions (e.g., HIV status, drug reactions)
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language

libraryUpgrade.imagePolicy: integrated patient-own EKG read (long QT) plus screening CXR
